content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
April 8, 1997
Thomas B. Brown
City Attorney
City ofNapa
955 School Street
Post Office Box 660
Napa, California 94559-0660
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-122
Dear Mr. Brown:
This letter is a response to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember Harry
Martin regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTIONS
1. Does Councilmember Martin’s relationship with and/or business interest in the Napa
Sentinel, and receipt of advertising income from the Napa City Employees Association League
(“NCEAL”) require Councilmember Martin’s disqualification from all labor negotiations and
decision-making concerning the Napa City Employees Association (“NCEA”) Memorandum of
Understanding (“MOU”)?
2. If Councilmember Martin is isqualified from participation and decision-making in
connection with the NCEA MOU, is he also disqualified from participation and decision-making
in connection with the MOUs for other bargaining units, including the Association of
Administrative and Managerial Employees, Napa Police Officers Association-Mid Management
and Napa City Firefighters Association?
3. Does Councilmember Martin’s relationship with and/or business interest in the Napa
Sentinel, coupled with his receipt of advertising income from the Napa Garbage Service (“NGS”),
disqualify him from participation and decision-making in connection with the establishment ofthe
City’s garbage rates?
1 Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-122
Page 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. Yes. Councilmember Martin may not participate in decisions that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on NCEAL or NCEA, including the labor
negotiations with NCEA.
2. We do not have enough facts to determine if Councilmember Martin is disqualified
from participating in labor negotiations with other bargaining units. He is only prohibited from
participating in those negotiations ifthey will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on NCEA, the NGS or any other economic interest of Councilmember Martin.
3. Yes. Councilmember Martin is disqualified from participating in decisions regarding
the garbage collection rate setting.
FACTS
The Two City Council Decisions
A.
Labor Negotiations And MOU Ratification.
The Napa City Council will be negotiating with the City’s designated employee bargaining
groups, including the NCEA over the next several months in an effort to enter into a new MOU
governing the terms and conditions of the various units’ employment. This process will require
the city council to meet with its designated labor negotiators in closed session, during which the
council, and the individual councilmembers, will discuss the labor contracts with the unions.
Thereafter, the city council likely will be asked to approve the negotiated MOUs in open session.
The MOU which sets the salaries ofNCEA employees, depending on negotiations, will likely
involve hundreds ofthousands of dollars.
At or about the same time as the negotiating and decision-making on the NCEA MOU
takes place, the city council also will be involved in negotiating MOUs for other city negotiating
units including the Association of Administrative and Managerial Employees, the Napa Police
Officers Mid-Management Association, and the Napa City Firefighters Association. Often terms
and conditions established in the course ofthe negotiations ofthe MOUs for one of these units
will be applied also for other units.
B.
Garbage Collection Rate Setting.
The city council also will be presented with a variety of issues leading to the annual
establishment of garbage collection rates. Under that process, the city reviews a rate application
submitted by the city’s garbage collection franchise holder, the Napa Garbage Service (“NGS”).


File No. A-97-122
Page 3
This process typically begins in June or July of each year and leads, in September or October, to a
public hearing concerning garbage rates. At the end ofthe public hearing process, the city council
votes to establish garbage collection rates for the following year. Again, depending on the
outcome, the setting of rates involves substantial dollar amounts totaling millions of dollars for
NGS.
Coundilmember Martin’s Business And Possible “Source of Income”
You are requesting advice on behalf of Councilmember Martin as to whether he may
participate in these two matters. The request arises from Councilmember Martin’s relationship
with a local Napa newspaper known as the Napa Sentinel.
The Sentinel is operated by a corporation known as H.V.M. Publishing, Inc.
Councilmember Martin is President and CEO ofH.V.M. Publishing, Inc., and manages and
exclusively controls the Sentinel on a day-to-day basis. Councilmember Martin is generally
regarded as the editor and publisher ofthe Sentinel and designates himself as publisher in his
Statements of Economic Interests. Councilmember Martin does not personally own any ofthe
stock in H.V.M. Publishing, Inc. Rather, all stock in the corporation is owned, in equal one-third
shares, by Councilmember Martin’s wife, his mother, and his adult daughter.
On February 21, 1997, NCEAL, a sponsored committee associated with the NCEA,
placed an advertisement in the Sentinel that cost in excess of $1,000. The Napa Police Officers
Association placed a similarly-priced election advertisement in March 1996. The NOS from time
to time places advertisements in the Sentinel and Councilmember Martin anticipates that NGS will
continue to run advertisements in the Sentinel over the next few months that will cost in excess of
$1,000.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A “public official” is defined in Section 82048 as an
individual who is a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local government
agency.


File No. A-97-122
Page 4
Economic Interests
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable
2 that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
“(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a
direct or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000)
or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than
loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
***
For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest
means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent
child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official,
or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official’s
agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or
beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater.” (Section 87103.)
According to your facts, Coundilmember Martin has received more than $250 in income
from the Sentinel in the past 12 monthsMoreover, Councilmember Martin’s spouse holds a
33.3-percent interest in H.V.M. Publishing, Inc. Assuming that interest is worth more than
$1,000, her investment interest is considered an indirect investment interest of Councilmember
Martin’s. Therefore, Councilmember Martin may not make any decisions that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on either the Sentinel or H.V.M. Publishing, Inc.
3
2 Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental
decision is made depends upon the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if
there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
You have not provided specific facts regarding the relationship between the Sentinel and H.V.M.
Publishing, Inc. It appears that the Sentinel is not a separate business entity from H.V.M. Publishing, Inc. If it
were, it appears that the Sentinel has a parent-subsidiary relationship with H.V.M. Publishing, Inc. A parent-


File No. A-97-122
Page 5
The definition of income includes the pro rata share of any income of any business entity in
which the official has a direct or indirect investment interest of 10-percent or more. (Section
82030(a).) We have determined that Councilmember Martin has an 33.3-percent indirect
investment in H.V.M. Publishing, Inc. Therefore, any income received by H.V.M. Publishing,
Inc. is also considered income to Councilmember Martin if Councilmember Martin’s 33.3-percent
share ofthe income exceeds $250 in the last 12 months.
In the past, for conflict-of-interest purposes, the Commission has “pierced” through
entities to consider the nature ofthe relationship between a person and an entity. In two opinions,
the Commission has pierced through the corporate veil and treated the controlling shareholder as
one with a closely held corporation. (In re Lumsdon (1972) 2 FPPC Ops. 140; In re Kahn (1975)
2 FPPC Ops. 151.) In In re Nord (1983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6, the Commission pierced through the
veil of a limited partnership and determined that the controlling general partner was one with the
limited partnership. The Commission reasoned that a limited partnership involves an investment
by the limited partners in the entrepreneurial skills of the general partner who has the sole
discretion and authority to manage the investment when conducting the partnership business.
In the Doyle Advice Letter, No. A-97-071, we applied the same rationale to candidates
and their controlled committees. A candidate has significant discretion in connection with the
malcing of expenditures by his or her controlled committee. (Section 82016.) Therefore, in that
letter, we treated a candidate and his controlled committee as one person for purposes of
analyzing a source of income as defined in Section 87 103(c).
This analysis also translates to a sponsoring organization and its sponsored committee.
An organization sponsors a committee ifthe sponsor provides 80-percent or more ofthe
committee’s contributions, collects contributions for the committee by use of payroll deductions
or dues, provides all or nearly all ofthe administrative services for the committee, or sets the
policies for soliciting contributions or making expenditures. (Section 82048.7.) Thus, a
sponsoring organization either provides much ofthe committee’s financial support or it sets policy
with regard to soliciting contributions or making expenditures. In either case, the sponsoring
organization has significant influence on the actions or decisions ofthe sponsored committee.
Accordingly, we will treat a sponsoring organization and its sponsored committee as one person
for purposes of analyzing a source of income as defined in Section 87103(c). H.V.M. Publishing,
Inc. received income in excess of $250 from the NCEAL in February 1997, a sponsored
committee associated with the Napa City Employees Association, thereby making the NCEAL a
source of income to H.V.M. Publishing, Inc., and Councilmember Martin. Councihnember
Martin may not participate in any governmental decision that will have a material financial effect
on either NCEAL or NCEA.
subsidiaiy relationship exists when one corporation possesses more than 50-percent of the voting power of another
corporation. (Regulation 18236.)


File No. A-97-122
Page 6
You mentioned that the Napa Police Officers Association placed an advertisement in the
Sentinel in March 1996. Income received is considered potentially disqua1ifring income by the
Act for only 12 months. (Section 82030(a).) Once 12 months has elapsed from the date the
income was received, it will no longer pose a conflict.
You also mentioned that the Sentinel received income from NGS in excess of $250 in the
past 12 months. Therefore, Coundilmember Martin may not participate in any decision that will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on NGS.
Materiality
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable ifthere is a substantial lilcelihood that it will occur. Certainty is
not required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
The standard for materiality differs depending on whether the investment interest or
source of income is directly or indirectly involved in the decision. Generally, ifthe official’s
economic interest is directly involved in a decision, the effect is deemed to be material.
A
business is directly involved in a decision when the business entity, or its agent: (1) initiates the
proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or; (2) is a named party in, or the subject of the proceeding concerning the decision
before the official or the official’s agency; (3) a person or business entity is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any
license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity.
(Regulation 18702.2(b).)
The city council will be negotiating with several employee bargaining groups, including
the NCEA to enter into new employmiit contracts with the public employees. The NCEA will be
a party to the employment contract negotiated with NCEA. Accordingly, the NCEA will be
directly involved in the decisions regarding the contract and thus the effect of the decisions will be
deemed material. The city council will also review a rate application submitted by NGS. NGS, a
source of income to Councilmember Martin, is the city’s garbage collection franchise holder.
From the facts you have provided, it appears that NGS will be the subject and named party in the
rate application. Coundilmember Martin may not participate in the rate application process.
You mentioned that the city will also be negotiating contracts with other city negotiating
units. Often terms and conditions established in the course ofthe negotiations for one contract
will also be applied to other contracts. From your facts, it does not appear that NCEA will be
involved in contract negotiations with other city employee negotiating units, however, some of
the terms decided upon in one contract negotiation may affect the NCEA negotiations. Thus, it


File No. A-97-122
Page 7
appears that NCEA may be indirectly involved in these negotiations because the negotiations may
have a financial effect on NCEA. Assuming the NCEA is a nonprofit organization, the standard
for materiality depends on the size ofthe organization. (Regulation 18702.5.) For an entity
whose gross annual receipts are $100,000 or less, the effect of a decision is material if
“(1) The decision will result in an increase or decrease ofthe
entity’s gross annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of
$10,000 or more.
(2) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid additional
expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $2,500 or more.
(3) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of the entity’s assets or liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or
more.” (Regulation 18702.5(f).)
You have not provided facts about the size of the NCEA. Also, we do not have enough
facts to determine whether the NCEA will be affected at all by decisions involving other employee
bargaining units, Therefore, you must make the final factual determination based on the above
standard.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
neral Counsel
By:
Liane Randolph
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:LR:ak

or more.

Sentinel, and receipt of advertising income from the Napa City Employees Association League
(“NCEAL”) require Councilmember Martin’s disqualification from all labor negotiations and
decision-making concerning the Napa City Employees Association (“NCEA”) Memorandum of
Understanding (“MOU”)?

connection with the NCEA MOU, is he also disqualified from participation and decision-making
in connection with the MOUs for other bargaining units, including the Association of
Administrative and Managerial Employees, Napa Police Officers Association-Mid Management
and Napa City Firefighters Association?
3. Does Councilmember Martin’s relationship with and/or business interest in the Napa
Sentinel, coupled with his receipt of advertising income from the Napa Garbage Service (“NGS”),
disqualify him from participation and decision-making in connection with the establishment ofthe
City’s garbage rates?

(916) 322-5660

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes. Councilmember Martin may not participate in decisions that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on NCEAL or NCEA, including the labor
negotiations with NCEA.
2. We do not have enough facts to determine if Councilmember Martin is disqualified
from participating in labor negotiations with other bargaining units. He is only prohibited from
participating in those negotiations ifthey will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on NCEA, the NGS or any other economic interest of Councilmember Martin.
3. Yes. Councilmember Martin is disqualified from participating in decisions regarding
the garbage collection rate setting.
